title: Warping a frame loom
subtitle: Setting up a warp for plain weave on a simple wooden frame
excerpt: A frame loom is warped by winding yarn continuously around the frame pegs to create a set of parallel warp threads. This tutorial covers measuring the warp to a consistent sett, tying off at the correct tension, and inserting a shed stick before weaving begins.
sourceNotes: Frame loom warping is a fundamental entry point for handweaving. The peg-warping method is standard for small frame looms. Observation-led.

content.text: A frame loom has pegs or notches at the top and bottom. You wind the
yarn continuously around the top and bottom pegs to create parallel threads. The
is determined by how many warp threads fit across the pegs. For plain weave in DK yarn, aim for 6-8 warp threads per 5 cm. The
inserted after warping creates the two sheds needed for plain weave without a heddle.

items.qty: enough to wind the full width, allow 3x the loom height for warp length
items.name: Warp yarn (a smooth, non-elastic yarn: cotton, linen, or worsted wool)
Stick shuttle (for the shed stick and for weaving)

content.text: Step 1: Tie on and begin winding

Tie the warp yarn to the top-left peg or notch with a secure knot. Wind down and around the bottom-left peg, back up and over the next top peg, down and around the next bottom peg, and so on across the frame from left to right. Keep tension consistent as you wind: the warp threads should feel firm but not stretched. Floppy threads produce uneven weaving.
After winding 5 cm of warp, count how many threads span that width. For DK

yarn, 6-8 warp threads per 5 cm gives a fabric in which both warp and weft are visible (a balanced plain weave). Fewer threads (open sett) will show more weft. More threads (close sett) will produce a warp-faced fabric.

When the full width is warped, tie the warp yarn to the last peg with the same tension as the starting end. Do not cut the yarn: wind any excess into a small butterfly and secure it for later use as weft if the colour works.

Weave a stick shuttle or flat ruler over and under alternate warp threads across the full width (over thread 1, under thread 2, over thread 3, and so on). Push the shed stick down toward the centre of the loom. When turned on its edge, this stick creates the first shed for weaving. The second shed is created by lifting alternate threads with a pick-up stick or your fingers for each return pass.

attrs.intro: Two problems in frame loom warping.

items.fix: Wind slowly and steadily. If the warp is already on the loom, slide a dowel under the slack threads and wedge it to equalise tension. Severely uneven warps are best cut and re-wound.
items.cause: Tension varied during winding, either from speed changes or from the ball of yarn running through fingers at inconsistent drag.
items.symptom: Warp threads are uneven in tension: some tight, some slack

items.fix: Check that the shed stick is woven over-under alternating threads and not skipping any. A thinner shed stick (a ruler rather than a thick dowel) turns more easily in a close-sett warp.
items.cause: The warp is too tightly sett for the shed stick thickness, or the threads are not separated over-under cleanly.
items.symptom: Shed stick is very hard to turn on its edge

attrs.heading: Common problems
